topStories.headline: Fisherman's Wharf Modernization Stirs Debate Among Commercial Fishermen
topStories.dek: As the Port of San Francisco's $10 million Fisherman's Wharf Forward project nears its summer 2026 completion target, commercial fishermen worry the transformation could marginalize the working waterfront.
topStories.body:
Fisherman's Wharf is undergoing a sweeping transformation as part of the Port of San Francisco's multi-phase Fisherman's Wharf Forward program. The near-term phase includes a new public plaza on Taylor Street where the former Alioto's restaurant once stood, a new inner lagoon overlook along Al Scoma Way, and upgraded lighting in the Inner Lagoon — all scheduled for completion by summer 2026.

But not everyone is celebrating. As ABC7 reported this week, commercial fishermen worry the changes will push them further to the margins of a waterfront that was once the heart of their industry. John Barnett, president of the Crab Boat Owners' Association, told ABC7 that navigating local roads with trailers full of gear has become increasingly difficult amid increased foot traffic and Waymo vehicles. There is currently no functioning fuel dock — a diesel leak in 2020 forced its closure, and cleanup is still underway. The Port says the fuel dock is expected to be operating again by spring 2027.

Meanwhile, some residents are leading an effort to rename the new plaza after the Alioto family, which first established a fish stand at the wharf over 100 years ago. The SF Examiner reported that Nunzio Alioto opened the business 101 years ago. The new plaza will feature views of the Golden Gate Bridge, upgraded lighting, seating, and landscaping.

The Fisherman's Wharf Community Benefit District also announced that a new Entertainment Zone — allowing open-air alcohol consumption between Polk and Powell streets and Beach Street to the Little Embarcadero — has been approved and aims to launch in May with a chowder competition. A weekly pop-up fish market at Al Scoma Way, which began in January, has averaged 77 customers per week.

topStories.headline: Vaillancourt Fountain Dismantled at Embarcadero Plaza Amid Fire Incident and Legal Battle
topStories.dek: Crews used cranes to remove massive concrete arms from the controversial Brutalist fountain this week as part of a $40 million plaza renovation — but a fire during deconstruction and an ongoing lawsuit have added drama to the project.
topStories.body:
The decades-long debate over the Vaillancourt Fountain at Embarcadero Plaza entered a dramatic new chapter this week as crews used cranes to dismantle the structure's massive concrete arms, some weighing up to 10 tons. The 710-ton Brutalist fountain, designed by Québécois artist Armand Vaillancourt and installed in 1972, is being removed as part of a broader $40 million renovation of Embarcadero Plaza and Sue Bierman Park.

The dismantling took a surprising turn when the fountain caught fire during the deconstruction process, as reported by The Art Newspaper and Artforum. Friends of the Plaza, a preservation group that has been fighting to save the fountain, raised safety concerns about the removal process, claiming workers appeared to lack proper protective equipment despite the city's previous assertions that the structure contained asbestos and lead.

The San Francisco Recreation and Park Department has said the fountain is being removed due to significant public safety risks from deterioration, including structural instability and widespread corrosion. The fountain's pieces will be placed in storage for up to three years while the city determines its future. A bench trial on the preservation lawsuit is scheduled for August.

The project is part of a sweeping effort to merge Embarcadero Plaza with Sue Bierman Park, creating a new five-acre park. City officials have unveiled five possible designs for the redeveloped space, which would include new green space and recreational features.

topStories.headline: 'Chonkers' the Giant Sea Lion Becomes Pier 39 Celebrity — Then Vanishes
topStories.dek: A 2,000-pound Steller sea lion nicknamed Chonkers drew massive crowds to Fisherman's Wharf for nearly two months before apparently departing — and an impostor briefly took his place.
topStories.body:
A massive Steller sea lion nicknamed Chonkers became San Francisco's biggest viral sensation this spring after first appearing at Pier 39's floating docks on March 13, 2026. Weighing roughly 2,000 pounds — about twice the size of the California sea lions that have called the pier home since 1989 — Chonkers quickly became a social media sensation and tourist magnet, drawing crowds of more than a hundred people at a time by April.

The nickname, a riff on internet slang for endearingly chunky animals, was coined by a San Francisco-based Reddit user. The phenomenon earned coverage from The Wall Street Journal, Smithsonian Magazine, the Associated Press, and ABC News, with Mayor Daniel Lurie even posting about him on Instagram. Pier 39 harbormaster Sheila Chandor told The New York Times that Chonkers has been visiting the pier for about 15 years, but typically stayed only a day or two.

This week, however, the San Francisco Chronicle reported that Chonkers appears to have departed — and a very large California sea lion unwittingly duped visitors by taking his usual spot. Pier 39 spokesperson Sue Muzzin confirmed the photographed animal was not Chonkers. Marine experts had said he was likely drawn south by the abundance of anchovies, herring, and rockfish in San Francisco Bay. Visitors hoping for a sighting can still check the Pier 39 Sea Lion Live Camera.

events.title: Bay to Breakers 2026
events.location: Start: Howard St. & Main St. (Embarcadero); Finish: Great Highway at Ocean Beach
events.description: San Francisco's iconic 12K and 15K costumed race, a tradition since 1912, passes through nine neighborhoods. Expect street closures and festive crowds along the Embarcadero start area.

events.title: Dear San Francisco — Immersive Circus Show at Club Fugazi
events.location: Club Fugazi, 678 Green Street, North Beach
events.description: The critically acclaimed 90-minute immersive acrobatic show by The 7 Fingers celebrates San Francisco's history through hoop diving, Chinese pole, juggling, and more. Tickets from $59.

events.title: Fisherman's Wharf Pop-Up Fish Market
events.description: Weekly pop-up market featuring fresh local catches including salmon, rockfish, and halibut, operating in conjunction with off-the-boat sales. Launched in January 2026 and averaging 77 customers per week.

events.title: Monroe's 'Battle of the Decades' DJ Night
events.description: Weekly DJ party and happy hour featuring progressive tunes spanning different decades each hour, with drink specials starting at $1.

events.title: North Beach First Fridays Art Crawl
events.location: Various galleries, museums, bars, and restaurants in North Beach
events.description: On the first Friday of every month, over a dozen North Beach venues stay open late for a self-guided art crawl showcasing local collections and entertainment.

events.title: 72nd Annual North Beach Festival
events.location: North Beach — between Filbert St. & Broadway, and Stockton St. & Grant Ave.
events.description: San Francisco's beloved street festival returns for Father's Day Weekend with 200+ vendors across 11 blocks, live entertainment, four beer gardens, a Blessing of the Animals, interactive art activities, and a kids' Fun Zone. Free admission.

events.title: Pier 39 Sea Lion Viewing
events.location: Pier 39 Sea Lion Center, Fisherman's Wharf
events.description: Sea lions have called Pier 39 home since 1989. The recent viral celebrity Chonkers may have departed, but hundreds of California sea lions remain. Check the Pier 39 Sea Lion Live Camera before visiting.

moreNews.headline: Fisherman's Wharf Hits One Million Monthly Visitors for First Time Since Pandemic
moreNews.body:
For the first time since the pandemic, Fisherman's Wharf recorded one million visitors in a single month (March 2026), representing a 24% increase compared to the same period in 2024, according to the San Francisco Examiner. However, the neighborhood continues to grapple with its reputation — for the second consecutive year, it earned the most 'tourist trap' mentions in global online reviews, according to travel data provider Nomad eSIM.

Fisherman's Wharf Community Benefit District executive director Bri Maughan pushed back on the label, calling the wharf a dynamic, evolving neighborhood where legacy businesses stand alongside new ones.

moreNews.headline: Northern Embarcadero Set for Safety Outreach in 2026
moreNews.body: The Northern Embarcadero — north of Broadway through Fisherman's Wharf — will undergo additional public outreach and review in 2026 as part of a quick-build effort and the forthcoming Embarcadero Connectivity Plan, according to the SFMTA. The Central Embarcadero Safety Project, covering Broadway to Brannan Streets, has reached its 65% design milestone and is targeting a construction start of fall 2026.

moreNews.headline: TEL HI Neighborhood Center Closed May 25 for Memorial Day
moreNews.body: The Telegraph Hill Neighborhood Center (TEL HI) will be closed on Monday, May 25, 2026, in observance of Memorial Day. The center, which serves families on Telegraph Hill and surrounding neighborhoods, recently partnered with Ben & Jerry's for its 15th Annual Free Cone Day and has been providing support to families affected by the SFUSD labor disruption.
